Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time to study, but it never really stuck to a schedule. I'd usually end up cramming things in between my extracurricular activities and whatever else was going on. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, other weeks it felt like I was just going through the motions. I'm a hands-on learner, so sitting and reading wasn't really my thing anyway.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd be pretty chill most of the time, then suddenly realize something was due and kick into gear. Regular times? Not really my style. Life just gets in the way too much, and honestly, I found it hard to care enough to stick to a rigid schedule when the material wasn't grabbing me.
Q3: Usually it was because I knew an assignment was coming up or I'd get a notification about something. Sometimes a classmate would message me asking about something and I'd realize I hadn't checked in for a while. It wasn't like I woke up excited to log in or anything. It was more of a necessity thing, like checking if there was anything I absolutely couldn't ignore.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. It never became automatic for me. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more often, especially when my peer support group was active and people were posting stuff. But calling it a routine would be a stretch. It always felt like an obligation rather than something that fit naturally into my day.
Q5: The discussion boards were actually decent because I could interact with other students and that made things less boring. I also found videos helpful since I could watch them while doing other things or rewind parts I didn't get. Having peers explain concepts in their own words sometimes clicked better than the formal lectures. The hands-on practice activities were good too when they actually existed.
Q6: The long reading materials? Yeah, I barely touched those. I'd skim them at best. Anything that felt like busy work or wasn't directly tied to an assignment got pushed aside. I have limited resources so I couldn't always print things out, and reading long PDFs on my phone was painful. If it wasn't immediately relevant, I just didn't prioritize it.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to something I needed to submit, it felt worth it. Also, if it was interactive or involved doing something rather than just reading or listening passively. When my peers were engaged with something and talking about it, that made me more likely to actually pay attention. Boredom is my enemy, so anything that broke the monotony helped.
Q8: Optional stuff felt like a trap honestly. Like, they say it's optional but then you feel guilty for not doing it, or it shows up on an exam anyway. But also, when I was already struggling to care about the required stuff, optional activities were the first to go. Required things had consequences attached, so they got done. Optional things just... didn't happen most of the time.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download slides and PDFs and study them on my phone during commutes or breaks. I also used YouTube a lot to find explanations that made more sense to me. My peer support group had a WhatsApp chat where we'd share notes and quiz each other. A lot of my actual learning happened in those spaces, not on the LMS itself.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be working through problems with friends in person or over video call, and none of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd be reviewing downloaded materials for hours but the LMS would just show I logged in once. My study group sessions were probably my most productive learning time, but there's no trace of that online.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once and then never go back to the LMS for it. I'd have it saved on my phone and reference it multiple times. The LMS probably showed one view, but I might have actually looked at that material five or six times offline. It was just easier than dealing with slow internet or the clunky platform.
Q12: Around midterms it got really hard. I was stressed, my anxiety was creeping up, and the material felt increasingly disconnected from anything I cared about. My extracurriculars were demanding more time, and I was just... bored. Like, deeply uninterested. When you're bored and stressed at the same time, engaging with coursework feels nearly impossible.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or overwhelmed, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. If I had competing responsibilities, I'd do the bare minimum online and focus my energy elsewhere. Confusion made me avoid certain modules entirely because I didn't have the mental energy to figure them out. My resources are limited too, so sometimes practical stuff just took priority.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a video and realize at the end I hadn't absorbed anything. It's like my body was going through the motions but I wasn't actually present. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though technically I was 'engaged' with the platform.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts and optional readings constantly. Even some assignments I knew were important got pushed until the last minute. Part of it was boredom, part was anxiety about not doing it perfectly. Sometimes I'd avoid things because I didn't understand them and didn't want to face that. It's easier to ignore something than admit you're struggling.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active on the LMS. Without that pressure, I'd drift. It's not healthy, but that urgency was the only thing that cut through the boredom. My usage probably looked like nothing, nothing, nothing, then a spike right before something was due.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it gave me a brief boost. Like, okay, maybe I can do this. But it wore off quickly. Negative or confusing feedback made me want to disengage even more. The peer support helped more than formal feedback honestly. Having someone say 'hey, you explained that well' in our group chat meant more than a grade sometimes.
Q18: The structure was okay but kind of rigid. Having four online courses meant everything blurred together. The weekly modules helped me know what was expected, but they also felt repetitive. I needed more variety, more hands-on stuff. The structure didn't account for different learning styles, so I had to adapt things myself to stay even slightly engaged.
Q19: The lack of interactive or practical activities made engagement harder. Everything felt very passive. When there were group components or peer discussions built in, that helped. But too much of it was just 'read this, watch that, submit something.' The platform itself was clunky too, which added friction. Small annoyances add up when you're already not motivated.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for things, trying to understand, clicking around but not finding what they need. Or they're anxious and checking obsessively without actually producing work. Maybe they're also doing what I do - using the LMS to access stuff but doing the real work elsewhere. High activity doesn't mean productive activity.
Q21: That's basically me. I didn't log in much, but I got a Merit. I studied offline a lot, worked with my peer group, used external resources. The LMS was just one piece of how I learned, and honestly not the biggest piece. You can engage with material deeply without the platform capturing any of it. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Logs can show patterns - when you're active, what you click on, time spent. But they miss everything qualitative. They don't know if I was actually paying attention or just had a tab open. They don't capture offline study, group work, or real understanding. They show behavior but not learning. It's like measuring exercise by counting gym check-ins.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and being mentally present. It's not just logging in or completing tasks - it's being curious, asking questions, connecting ideas. For me, engagement happens when something interests me or when I'm working with others. It's an internal state that external metrics can't really capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group carried me through this course. It wouldn't show the anxiety I managed or the boredom I fought against. It wouldn't capture the offline hours, the group chats, the moments of genuine understanding that happened away from the screen. My engagement was real, just not always visible in the data.
